name: GLYPH FIELD - cursor-proximity variable-font specimen grid
ref: Exat Typeface microsite by HotType (CSSDA WOTY 2025 nominee, Codrops case study April 2026 - tympanus.net/codrops/2026/04/10/the-exat-microsite); Obys Agency kinetic typography (metabole.studio immersive 2026 round-up)
palette: #0d0d0d (near-black) + #f0ece4 (bone glyph fill); proximity rings run ice-blue #90c8f0 at distance to deep red #c0392b at pointer
brief: The entire first viewport is a dense grid of repeated lowercase glyphs set in a single variable font. As the cursor moves, glyphs within 7 concentric distance-rings shift their weight axis and hue in real time - hairline at the periphery, ultra-black at the cursor's center - creating a cursor-shaped pressure depression in the text field. Works are hidden beneath the glyph layer and revealed by clicking a glyph cluster, which parts the field like a curtain. Distinct from word-level kinetic type (morph): it operates at individual-glyph scale with Euclidean distance math, no animation timeline. The glyph-field pressure depression is visible at scroll=0 as soon as the cursor enters. Mobile: device tilt (DeviceOrientation) drives the ring center instead of the cursor; works are revealed by tap-and-hold. Performance guardrail: glyph positions are in a fixed DOM grid updated by CSS custom properties per cell via a single rAF loop; no WebGL required. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: BLIND WIPE - SVG mask reveal gallery (4 wipe patterns)
ref: Codrops 'SVG Mask Transitions on Scroll with GSAP and ScrollTrigger' (March 11 2026 - tympanus.net/codrops/2026/03/11/svg-mask-transitions); Cyd Stumpel Portfolio 2025 CSS View Transitions (Awwwards SOTD March 2025)
palette: #f4f0e8 (bone reveal surface) + #111111 (mask black) + #d8482a (vermilion seam accent visible mid-transition)
brief: Each work in the gallery is hidden behind a full-bleed SVG mask; scrolling to it triggers one of four wipe patterns in rotation: (1) horizontal blinds - paired rectangles open from center lines up and down; (2) random grid - rectangular panels open at shuffled timings; (3) vertical blinds - strips expand outward left and right like a venetian blind; (4) column wave - a left-to-right wave with random panel openings within each column. At scroll=0 the first work is mid-wipe already so the signature mechanic is immediately visible. Distinct from glitch-datamosh (RGB/VHS aesthetic) and fold-origami (paper fold geometry). Mobile: all four patterns work identically on touch-scroll via IntersectionObserver. Guardrail: SVG masks are hardware-accelerated; no canvas or WebGL needed; reduced-motion collapses to instant opacity reveal. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: IMAGE TUBE - scroll-driven infinite 3D image cylinder
ref: Codrops 'Reactive Depth: Scroll-Driven 3D Image Tube with React Three Fiber' (Feb 17 2026 - tympanus.net/codrops/2026/02/17/reactive-depth-building-a-scroll-driven-3d-image-tube); Lusion studio Three.js inertia + Z-axis depth (lusion.co)
palette: #050505 (void) + #c8f53e (lime accent for UI chrome); images supply all interior color
brief: Works are arranged as image planes on the outer face of a 3D cylinder using trigonometry (each plane faces outward). Scrolling adds rotational velocity to the cylinder; releasing lets inertia carry it and dampen. Hovering slows the entire system. The front-facing plane is the only one fully sharp and lit; side planes curve away in perspective blur. An infinite seamless loop resets the cylinder's Y position at the halfway mark so there is no hard stop. The background hosts a deforming grid plane (vertex shader, smoothstep edge/center mask) that ripples with mouse position. Distinct from ring-carousel (CSS preserve-3d fan, no WebGL, no inertia) and coverflow-3d (flat cards on Y-axis). The inertia spin is live at scroll=0. Mobile: touch-drag spin replaces scroll; auto-slow on touch-end. WebGL note: avoid GLSL ES 1.0 dynamic-indexed local arrays (ANGLE bug returns 0); use texture LUTs instead; confirm canvas paints before starting the render loop. Guardrail: render at DPR 1; post-process blur is a single-pass shader, not per-element CSS. three.js via importmap allowed.

name: MOOD METER - scroll speed live-tunes ambient hue and blur
ref: Codrops 'Scroll-Reactive 3D Gallery with Three.js, Velocity, and Mood-Based Backgrounds' (March 9 2026 - tympanus.net/codrops/2026/03/09/building-a-scroll-reactive-3d-gallery); Terminal Industries REJOUICE viral scroll-speed speedometer (threads.net/@framer, Awwwards SOTM Sep 2025)
palette: #0a0a0a (base void); hue shifts dynamically: slow-scroll = icy cyan #a8d4f5, medium = amber #f4a000, fast = magenta #ff2d78; gallery cards stay near-white
brief: A velocity-reading system measures the derivative of scroll position each frame and maps it to a CSS custom property that drives the page's ambient hue, the backdrop blur radius around gallery cards, and a live ENERGY HUD gauge in the corner (thin arc + numeric BPM-style readout). Scrolling slowly keeps the palette cold and cards crisp; scrolling fast turns the background hot and cards hazy. Each work card is a frosted slab whose label weight also tracks velocity. Works are presented in a clean vertical gallery; the mood system is the differentiation, not the layout. At scroll=0 the HUD is live and the hue is cold, revealing the mechanic immediately. Distinct from arc (fixed palette per chapter) and time-of-day (clock-driven, not velocity-driven). Mobile: scroll velocity on touch behaves identically. Guardrail: velocity calc is a single rAF differential; no WebGL; all color transitions are CSS transitions on a custom property. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: WEBTOON - vertical comic-panel scroll where panels breathe
ref: Ponpon Mania by Patrick Heng / OKAY DEV (Awwwards SOTM October 2025, Codrops case study Oct 7 2025 - tympanus.net/codrops/2025/10/07/ponpon-mania); Siena Film Foundation filmstrip-slider by Niccolo Miranda (Awwwards SOTD March 2025)
palette: #fefcf6 (panel paper) + #1a1a1a (ink outline) + #e63946 (spot red accent for dialogue bubbles and action lines)
brief: Works are presented as vertical comic panels - each panel has an inked SVG border, a caption box in the upper-left corner (work title/prompt), and dialogue bubble annotations. As each panel enters the viewport it breathes: subtle CSS scale pulse, a speed-line radial sweep on IntersectionObserver, and the caption types itself in. The gutter between panels is intentionally white with a thin rule. Clicking a panel expands it manga-style (the panel border scales to fill the viewport with a perspective push). At scroll=0 the top panel is already breathing, revealing the mechanic in the first second. Distinct from manifesto-essay (prose column, inline figures) and zine-collage (cut-paste punk aesthetic with tape and torn paper). Mobile: single-column vertical strip matches the native webtoon format exactly. Guardrail: all panel animations are CSS + IntersectionObserver; zero WebGL; reduced-motion disables the pulse and speed-lines only. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: JOURNEY MAP - scroll draws a cinematic SVG path across a stylized map
ref: Codrops 'Creating Scroll-Driven SVG Map Animations with GSAP' (May 21 2026 - tympanus.net/codrops/2026/05/21/creating-scroll-driven-svg-map-animations); Floema Jewelry WebGL horizontal+vertical scroll hybrid (Awwwards SOTM May 2026 / burocratik)
brief: The hero is a full-viewport stylized SVG map (hand-drawn contour lines, minimalist place names in a condensed serif). A dashed parchment route line draws itself via stroke-dashoffset as the user scrolls; a small animated vehicle dot follows the path tip. At each work's waypoint the camera zooms into a golden marker using a CSS transform scale, and the work expands from the marker like an unfolded document. The entire narrative is spatial: the visitor is travelling through a creative atlas. Distinct from particle-constellation (star node graph, canvas pan-zoom, no path or map metaphor) and infinite-canvas (spatial board with no narrative direction). The route line begins drawing at scroll=0, revealing the mechanic immediately. Mobile: the map is SVG-responsive; camera zoom uses transform-origin set to each waypoint; pinch-zoom is disabled to preserve the scroll-driven narrative. Guardrail: all animation is scroll-listener driven on SVG stroke-dashoffset and CSS transform; no WebGL. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: UNFOLD GRID - sticky viewport unlocks cells progressively as you scroll
ref: Codrops 'Sticky Grid Scroll' (March 2 2026 - tympanus.net/Tutorials/StickyGridScroll); Dropbox Brand site (CSSDA WOTY 2025 nominee, staggered-grid reflow)
palette: #f3efe8 (bone) + #111 (ink) + #2c6fad (unlock blue accent for the reveal flash)
brief: The page has a single sticky layout container that fills the viewport and never scrolls away. Below the fold is a long scroll container whose progress drives a single CSS custom property. As that property increases, grid cells transition one-by-one from locked (blurred, desaturated, a padlock icon overlay) to unlocked (sharp, full color, padlock dissolves with a brief blue flash). The unlocking order spirals from center-outward, creating a growing circle of revealed work. When all cells are unlocked the layout un-sticks and scrolls normally into a detail panel. At scroll=0 all cells are locked except the center one (flashing), immediately demonstrating the mechanic. Distinct from morphing-grid (same cells reflow between formations) and bento (always fully visible). Mobile: 2-column grid; cells unlock top-to-bottom in reading order. Guardrail: lock state is a CSS class toggled by a single scroll listener; no canvas; blur is CSS backdrop-filter with GPU compositing. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: ENDLESS LOOP - seamless infinite parallax scroll with no page end
ref: Codrops 'The Never Ending Story: Seamless Infinite Scroll with GSAP and Lenis' (May 28 2026 - tympanus.net/codrops/2026/05/28/the-never-ending-story); Codrops 'Building an Infinite Parallax Grid with GSAP and Seamless Tiling' (June 2025)
palette: #06060a (deep void) + #ff6b35 (ember accent for loop indicator); works supply all midground color
brief: There is no page end. Works float as framed panels at three depth layers (foreground, midground, background) that each tile seamlessly: when the last item in any layer exits the viewport, it teleports to the front - invisible to the user - creating a perpetual orbit. A subtle LOOP counter in the corner increments each full cycle, the only signal that the scroll is circular. Snap-control gives the experience rhythm at each midground work. Distinct from parallax-depth (finite diorama with a scroll endpoint) and infinite-canvas (spatial board you pan freely, not a looping scroll lane). The three-layer parallax depth is live at scroll=0. Mobile: touch-scroll drives the same three-layer tiling; loop counter still shown. Guardrail: the tiling logic is a single rAF translate loop using modulo arithmetic on a CSS transform; no duplicated DOM nodes beyond the minimum tile set; no WebGL required. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: SOUNDSCAPE - audio-reactive equalizer gallery (mic or file input)
ref: Codrops 'Creating an Audio-Reactive Visual Experience with the Web Audio API' (tympanus.net/codrops/2024/03/05/audio-reactive-visual-web-audio-api); Active Theory audio-reactive WebGL demos (activetheory.net); Web Audio API frequency-bar visualizers widely featured on CSS Design Awards 2025-2026
palette: #080808 (void) + #00e5ff (cyan frequency bars) + #ff2d78 (magenta peak markers); bars themselves are the color system
brief: Works are arranged in a vertical gallery where each card has a real-time equalizer bar column beneath it. The bars respond to either (a) a microphone input via getUserMedia or (b) a short audio loop playing per card - frequency bands map bar heights using an AnalyserNode FFT. The tallest bar at any moment highlights the active card. Clicking a card locks the focus, dims the others, and plays a subtle ambient tone aligned to the equalizer. At scroll=0 the mic permission prompt fires immediately or a default ambient pulse runs, so the bars are live before any user scroll. Distinct from all existing templates: it is the only one where audio signal - not cursor or scroll - is the primary interaction driver. Mobile: mic prompt works on iOS/Android; reduced-motion only disables the bar scale animation, not the audio analysis. Guardrail: AnalyserNode smoothingTimeConstant = 0.85 to prevent jitter; canvas 2D draws bars at DPR 1; no WebGL. Implement with native CSS/JS Web Audio API, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: TUMBLE REEL - scroll-driven 3D image rotations along a wave path
ref: Codrops 'Exploring 3D Image Rotations on Scroll' (June 18 2026 - tympanus.net/codrops/2026/06/18/exploring-3d-image-rotations-on-scroll/); Lusion studio scroll-driven 3D reveal for Mercedes EQS (lusion.co)
palette: #0c0c0c (near-black) + #f0ece4 (bone image matte); accent is purely the images themselves, zero chrome color
brief: Works enter the viewport from off-screen and travel a sine-wave Z-depth path as they scroll: each image simultaneously translates on Y, rotates on X and Z axes, and scales - creating a tumbling, falling-card effect. Easing curves differ per image so no two feel identical. Images reach a rest state at the viewport center (flat, full-scale, fully sharp), then tumble out the other side. A scroll-progress indicator is the only persistent UI. Distinct from space (WebGL 3D fly-through with framed planes) and parallax-depth (diorama layers, no rotation). The first image is mid-tumble at scroll=0. Mobile: reduced rotation range (X-axis only) keeps it readable on small screens. Guardrail: all transforms are pure CSS transform3d updated via a single scroll listener and rAF; no WebGL; will-change: transform set per card; reduced-motion collapses to a simple vertical fade-in. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: X-RAY - cursor fluid-trail unmasks a hidden scene layer
ref: Codrops 'Building a Dual-Scene Fluid X-Ray Reveal Effect in Three.js' (March 23 2026 - tympanus.net/codrops/2026/03/23/building-a-dual-scene-fluid-x-ray-reveal-effect-in-three-js); Lusion creative studio WebGL post-processing (lusion.co)
palette: #050a12 (abyss) + #00f0c8 (bioluminescent teal for the revealed layer); outer scene is desaturated near-black, inner revealed scene is full-color or inverted
brief: Each gallery card renders two overlapping states: an outer (default) desaturated version and an inner (hidden) full-color or skeleton/wireframe version. As the cursor moves, a ping-pong fluid simulation accumulates the trail - the resulting organic mask is inverted and used to blend between the two states wherever the fluid is active. When the cursor stops, the fluid diffuses and fades, restoring the outer state over ~1 second. The fluid diffusion is the entire visual identity: no hover states, no click reveals, just living material response. Distinct from spotlight-noir (geometric radial falloff, no fluid physics) and liquid-distortion (morphs between works, not between dual states of one work). The fluid mask is active at scroll=0 as soon as the cursor enters. Mobile: tap-and-hold triggers a radial fluid burst centered on the touch point; auto-fades after 1.5 s. WebGL note: avoid GLSL ES 1.0 dynamic-indexed local arrays (ANGLE returns 0); use uniform arrays indexed by a loop variable only; confirm canvas paints before the render loop starts. Guardrail: render at DPR 1; ping-pong FBO at half resolution; mobile falls back to a CSS clip-path radial reveal on tap. three.js via importmap allowed for the fluid simulation only.

name: CORRIDOR - walkable 3D infinite corridor, hover paints sketches to color
ref: Codrops 'Sketching the Impossible: A 3D Portfolio Built Without a Single 3D Model' (June 11 2026 - tympanus.net/codrops/2026/06/11/sketching-the-impossible-a-3d-portfolio-built-without-a-single-3d-model/); GSAP Site of the Day 2026 (gsap.com/showcase)
palette: #f5f0e6 (sketchbook paper) + #1a1a1a (ink line); color appears only on hover-painted zones - no global accent hue
brief: The entire site is a first-person walkable 3D corridor built from flat textured planes (no 3D models). Scrolling moves the camera forward and backward through an infinite chunked corridor; mouse/gyroscope tilt adds parallax camera lean. Doorways branch off the corridor at intervals and each one, when approached, expands to reveal a work. The signature interaction: hovering any surface triggers a GLSL paint-fill shader that floods the ink sketch with color - this is the only navigation affordance (color = clickable). At scroll=0 the corridor is already visible in first-person perspective and the paint effect fires on first mouse-enter, demonstrating both mechanics within seconds. Distinct from space (WebGL fly-through of framed planes in Z-space, no corridor walls or paint reveal), orbit (hero model + bento, no walkable space), and infinite-canvas (2D pan/zoom board, no first-person perspective). Mobile: gyroscope drives the tilt; tap-and-hold triggers the paint fill instead of hover. WebGL note: avoid GLSL ES 1.0 dynamic-indexed local arrays (ANGLE returns 0); use sampler2D texture LUTs for the paint mask; confirm canvas paints before the render loop starts. Guardrail: corridor chunks are loaded lazily (IntersectionObserver on dummy sentinel divs); render at DPR 1; mobile disables the paint shader and uses a CSS tint overlay instead. three.js via importmap allowed.
